summary: DHH testifies before the House Antitrust Subcommittee about how Google, Apple, and Facebook have transformed the once-open internet into a marketplace controlled by monopolies. He argues Google extorts businesses by selling their trademarked names to competitors, Apple exploits its App Store duopoly with a 30% fee and draconian review processes, and Facebook's data vacuuming creates an inescapable advertising machine. He calls on Congress to intervene, arguing small companies have no agency to resist these forces and that the promise of a middleman-free internet has been broken.
key_points: Google leverages its search monopoly to force businesses to pay for their own trademarked brand names, while protecting its own trademarks automatically
Apple and Google's App Store duopoly sustains a 30% fee versus 2% in competitive credit-card markets, with draconian enforcement against alternatives
Facebook and Google captured 99% of internet ad growth by 2016, creating an inescapable surveillance advertising machine
Big tech's relentless expansion into every industry means all businesses will eventually compete against them
The acquisitions of Instagram and WhatsApp should never have been approved and need to be undone
money_quotes: The internet has been colonized by a handful of big tech companies that wield their monopoly power without restraint.
The promise that the internet was going to cut out the middleman has been broken.
We're all left to accept that these companies can and do alter the deal, any deal, however they please.
Google uses this monopoly to extort businesses like ours to pay for the privilege that consumers who search for our trademarked brand name can find us.
Google would never have been able to capture a monopoly in search by acting like this from the start.
Then you'll be stuck in an appeals process that would make Kafka blush.
Not even Putin would dare brag of an approval rating that high!
Help us, congress. You're our only hope.
key_insight: The open internet that enabled small companies to compete has been captured by a handful of monopolists who now serve as inescapable, exploitative middlemen — exactly what the internet was supposed to eliminate.
